narrative:
Your law school application was denied because our prediction model estimated a 67% chance that you would not pass the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic and background details. This threshold is set to prioritize admitting those most likely to succeed on the bar, helping the school maintain its strong outcomes. The model's decision hinged on three key factors from your profile: your undergraduate GPA, your first-year grade decile in school, and your LSAT score, which together painted a picture of higher risk compared to students who typically pass the bar.

Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 stood out as the strongest driver pushing the prediction toward bar failure—it's noticeably below the 3.24 average among those who passed, signaling to the model a foundational academic challenge that often correlates with bar struggles. Similarly, your first-year decile of 3 was the second-biggest influence in the same direction, well under the 6.04 average for successful bar takers, suggesting early performance hurdles that the model sees as hard to overcome. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 40 worked in your favor, above the 37.27 average for passers, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success and showing your potential in that area.

While aspects like your upper-middle family income align with many who pass (where it's the most common bracket) and your plan to attend full-time matches the norm for 93% of successful students, they didn't outweigh the top concerns. Overall, your profile compares to admitted students by lagging in those core academic metrics that the model weighs heavily, even with strengths like your LSAT. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to boost it further, or strengthening your GPA through a post-bac program or additional coursework to shift those deciles upward—many students in similar spots have succeeded by targeting these exact areas before reapplying. We encourage you to use this feedback as a roadmap; with focused improvements, your chances could change dramatically next time.
